Identity.History: Philip II of Macedon ruled from 359–336 BC and transformed Macedon from a vulnerable kingdom into the dominant power of Greece. A skilled soldier, diplomat, and reformer, he created the Macedonian phalanx, strengthened cavalry, and built a professional army. Through war, alliances, and marriage politics, he expanded Macedonian control and defeated the Greek city-states of Thebes and Athens at Chaeronea in 338 BC. He planned an invasion of Persia but was assassinated before it began. His achievements made possible the conquests of his son, Alexander the Great.
